Lemon Yogurt Cake

Preparation time : 15-20 minutes
Baking Time : 35-40 minutes.
Servings : 8
Ingredients:
- 1 serving of plain yogurt (125g)
- 3 eggs
- 250g of sugar
- 10g of vanilla sugar (1 pack)
-  300g of flour
- 60ml of sunflower oil
- 10 g of baking powder (1 pack)
- Zest of one lemon
- Juice of one lemon
Directions :
1. Preheat the oven to 180 °C
2. Mix the yogurt, eggs, sugar and vanilla sugar in a bowl.
3. Add the flour, baking powder, oil and lemon zest. Mix until you get a smooth dough.
4. Add the lemon juice and mix again.
5. Pour the dough into a greased and floured baking pan
6. Bake for 35 to 40 minutes until the cake turns chocolate brown
7. Cool it for 15-20 minutes
Enjoy your meal !

rewrite instyle of terry pratchet: In case I need to spell this out, no matter how many instagram posts, Facebook and Twitter there are, human writers can draw on experience in the real world to create art. AI can draw on what has been digitized, which is less than 100% of the real world, and always will be. That's why AI generative pictures right now can't get fingers right. It's why scripts are clumsy. Sure, we can imagine a continuous development where they fix the obvious errors in AI, but they'll never see the world other than through a digital lens. The uncanny valley will always open wide for this AI.

And, yes, eventually they will be good enough for many creative works that fall into narrow ranges of expectations. Sermons, college essays, daily sports reporting, sitcoms, police procedurals, etc. But make no mistake, AI work is skin deep. They can parrot behavior but not invent it still.

The issue for society is if we give up all notion of humanity, what will be left of us and the planet? If you don't find value in genuine human output vs digital, than why bother with anything else in nature? Or the future of the planet? It's all derivative work.

Please update this code so it only download images in horizontal size format

import os
import requests
import pypexels


def download_images(api_key, queries, per_page, save_dir, max_pages=5):
    # Initialize PyPexels client
    py_pexel = pypexels.PyPexels(api_key=api_key)

    # Loop through the queries
    for query in queries:
        # Create a directory for the query
        query_dir = os.path.join(save_dir, query.replace(' ', '_'))
        if not os.path.exists(query_dir):
            os.makedirs(query_dir)

        # Download images from multiple pages
        for page in range(1, max_pages + 1):
            # Make the API request
            search_results = py_pexel.search(query=query, page=page, per_page=per_page)

            # Download each image
            for i, photo in enumerate(search_results.entries):
                # Set the file name with the query and photo ID
                file_name = f'{query_dir}/{query}{i+1}_{photo.id}.jpg'

                # Download the image
                response = requests.get(photo.src.get('large2x'), stream=True)
                if response.status_code == 200:
                    with open(file_name, 'wb') as f:
                        for chunk in response:
                            f.write(chunk)

                    print(f"Downloaded {file_name}")
                else:
                    print(f"Failed to download {file_name}")

import requests
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup

url = "https://market.poizonshop.ru/?ysclid=lh3dvkwcpb735030874"
response = requests.get(url)

soup = BeautifulSoup(response.content, "html.parser")

items = soup.select(".js-store- grid-list>.t-store__grid-item")

for item in items:
    # Получаем название предмета
    title = item.select_one(".js-store-prod-name.js-product-name.t-store__card__title.t-name.t-name_md")
    if title:
        title = title.text.strip()

    # Получаем ссылку на фото
    img = item.select_one(".js-product-img.t-store__card__bgimg.t-store__card__bgimg_hover.t-bgimg.loaded")
    if img:
        img_url = img["style"].split("(", 1)[1].split(")")[0].strip()

    # Получаем ссылку на подробную информацию
    details_link = item.select_one(".store__card__wrap_txt-and-opts>a")
    if details_link:
        details_url = details_link["href"]

        # Получаем цену на странице с подробной информацией товара
        details_resp = requests.get(details_url)
        details_soup = BeautifulSoup(details_resp.content, "html.parser")

        price = details_soup.select_one(".js-product-price.js-store-prod-price-val.t-store__prod-popup__price-value.notranslate")
        if price:
            price = price.text.strip()

        # Получаем подробную информацию о товаре
        detailed_info = details_soup.select_one(".js-store-prod-text.t-store__prod-popup__text.t-descr.t-descr_xxs>div.js-store-prod-all-text>strong:nth-child(1)")
        if detailed_info:
            detailed_info = detailed_info.text.strip()

        print(f"Название товара: {title}")
        print(f"Ссылка на изображение: {img_url}")
        print(f"Ссылка на подробную информацию: {details_url}")
        print(f"Цена товара: {price}")
        print(f"Подробная информация: {detailed_info}")
        print("\n" + "="*30 + "\n")

The current class structure is as follows:

1. Core:

- class Engine
- This is the main class that will initialize, run, and shut down other subsystems of the engine (e.g., Window, Renderer, Input, etc.)

2. Window:

- class Window
- This class will handle window creation, resizing, and destruction using GLFW. It should also handle keyboard and mouse input events.

3. Renderer:

- class Renderer
- This class will manage Vulkan objects (e.g., instance, device, swapchain, command buffers) and rendering pipelines.
- class Shader
- To handle shader modules creation and destruction.
- class Texture
- To handle texture loading and destruction.
- class Pipeline
- To encapsulate the description and creation of a Vulkan graphics or compute pipeline.

4. Scene:

- class Scene
- This class will manage the list of objects in the vector, update their transforms, and send them to the Renderer for rendering.
- class GameObject
- To represent individual objects in the scene, store their transformation matrices, and bind the relevant data like vertices and indices.
- class Camera
- To store and manage camera properties such as position, rotation, view, and projection matrices.
- class Mesh
- To handle mesh data (e.g., vertices, indices, etc.) for individual objects.
- class Material
- To store and manage material properties such as colors, shaders, and textures.

5. Math:

- Utilize GLM library to handle vector and matrix math operations.

Introduction:

As you plan your trip to the breathtaking Virgin Islands, it’s essential to ensure that you have the necessary travel documents for a seamless journey. In this comprehensive guide, we will cover everything you need to know about securing a visa for airport transit in the Virgin Islands.

Understanding Airport Transit Visas:

An Airport Transit Visa (ATV) allows air travelers to transit through the international zone of an airport without entering the country. It is particularly useful if you have a layover in the Virgin Islands before continuing to another destination. Please note that an ATV is only valid for a maximum of 24 hours.

Who Requires an Airport Transit Visa?

It is crucial to determine whether you need an ATV or not for your layover in the Virgin Islands. Generally, if you are a citizen of a visa-exempt country or you hold a multiple-entry visa for the destination country, you are allowed to transit without an ATV.

However, nationals from certain countries still require an ATV when transiting through the Virgin Islands’ airports. It is advisable to check the Virgin Islands’ consulate or embassy in your home country for the most accurate and up-to-date information.

The fourth edition of Jonathan Riley-Smith’s book, What Were the Crusades? published by the Ignatius Press in March 23, 2009, is a self-described introduction to the crusading movements of the medieval period. The book primarily covers the First through Fifth Crusades to the Middle East, issued by the Catholic Church, with the primary objective being to (re)take Jerusalem and its surrounding territories. It also covers the French crown-decreed Sixth and Seventh crusades, also known as King Louis IX’s first and second crusades, respectively. It finely details the reasoning, justification, planning, and execution of the crusades, as well as the sociopolitical consequences of the crusades in Europe and the Middle East.

Riley-Smith is regarded as among the most influential crusading scholars in modern times and it is apparent in his writing. What Were the Crusades? possesses an extensive bibliography and the information itself is presented in an easily digestible manner, without sacrificing too much detail or giving the reader too little credit in his comprehension. While not perfect, no work is. It is overall an exemplary introduction to the crusades and of introductory reading in general.

How does Descartes prove the existence of God? Use these notes when answering the question in a detailed, concise, and excellent manner: Descartes proves God’s existence by using the principle of causation.
Descartes’ mind is made up of ideas and modes of thinking. 
Ideas are what we think about
When we do not refer our ideas to anything outside of us, we can know that the idea exists.
When we have ideas, we cannot deny that they exist.
Anything that exists is an effect.
According to the principle of causation, any effect implies a cause and any cause is as real as its effect.
Therefore, any time we have an idea, we know that the idea has been caused and the cause of this idea must be at least as real as its effect.
So, if we have an idea for which we know we are not the cause, then we know something else exists that must have caused it and is at least as real as it.
We have the idea of God, which by definition is infinitely powerful and infinitely knowledgable
If we were the cause of God, we would be infinitely powerful and infinitely knowledgable. 
If we were the cause of God, in other words, we would be God
We are not God
Therefore, we cannot have been the cause of our idea of God
But we cannot deny that the idea of God exists, so it must have been caused by God
Therefore, God exists.

Data analytics (DA) is becoming increasingly important in supply chain management (SCM) due to its ability to provide valuable insights that can improve efficiency and decision-making. One of the key applications of DA in SCM is demand forecasting, which involves predicting future demand for products or services. Accurate demand forecasting is crucial for ensuring that the right amount of inventory is maintained, reducing the risk of stockouts, and optimizing production and logistics processes.
There are several algorithms that can be used for demand forecasting in SCM, and they can be broadly classified into two categories: time-series forecasting and causal forecasting. Time-series forecasting algorithms rely on historical data to make predictions, while causal forecasting algorithms take into account external factors such as economic indicators, weather patterns, and market trends.
This study will Evaluate both time-series and casual algorithms and study their efficacy and uses.

The narrative voice of A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man is remarkable for its limited yet fluid third-person narrative style, which is closely aligned to the consciousness of Stephen Dedalus. We never get to see inside other characters’ heads; instead, we see them the way Stephen does.. The subjectivity of first-person narration (in the form of a diary at the end) is combined with the objectivity of third-person narration, resulting in a narrative voice that is capable of conveying Stephen’s experiences and thoughts with an immediacy and an intimacy that, until then, had been rarely achieved in the bildungsroman form. Indeed, the book focuses so closely on Stephen, and takes us so deeply into his mind, that at times it resembles a first-person narrative. By doing so, the inner life of Stephen seeks to delineate in such a way that it does not merely serve the unfolding of the story but becomes the story. The narrator presents Stephen’s consciousness and activities in various ways; an important aspect about his narration is that he persistently adapts his style to Stephen’s idiom and mood

For the first time the reader is exploring young child’s psychological struggles and tensions; a deep penetration into the processes of the soul, at such an intimate level. Child Stephen must navigate through the treacherous waters of conflicting forces- family, religion, school and national expectations- each of which attempts to define his sense of self and determine his ultimate destiny. Family is no longer viewed as a paradisiacal island where children are cosseted but a double- edged sword- a sword that can impose constraints on personal freedom and self-discovery

Good evening. My name is Jaye. I've written a three-part editorial essay. Please discuss it. Here is part one:
 
"Section A

'They say communism doesn't work; that's true. But, guess what else? Capitalism doesn't work, either. No system works; society doesn't work. Civilization doesn't work, because human beings don't work. Everything organic dies, and it ends up taking everything else with it, eventually, too. Do you want something to last? Then you'll have to build it yourself. But don't get too attached; recognize the inherent necessity in incompleteness, and the intrinsic contingency in completeness. There's a process to a state, and a state to a process. One day, you might tower above the clouds; the next, you're babelling about the past like some far-off neverland. 

Listen, you want some advice? Stay in the moment. Ground yourself to the presence of the Earth. Mark your discernment of the nature of the world with engaged action towards the sustainable restoration of all things. Bring a sense of a purpose to an otherwise unmeaning world, through the effectuation of your own salvation. The only light you need bask in is the light which birthed the world; find strength in the heart of the Sun, and grow roots toward the stillness in the Earth. There is peace in adaptation, but one must keep adapting, piece-by-piece, one step at a time.' 

-Synthesis of the ideas of Keaton Weiss, Russell Dobular, and myself."

Rewrite "Dairy production systems and productivity in Ethiopia
In Ethiopia, there are four major type of dairy production system available. These are agro-pastoralism, mixed crop-livestock, urban-peri urban and intensive system. The major (98%) national milk production (supply) is coming from the first two production systems; but low in terms of per cow milk production. The first two production systems are characterized by low-input and low out-put systems. The crossbreed dairy cows are introduced to the production system for the purpose of increasing the milk production per cow by crossbreeding program. Although the exotic dairy cows are introduced in these production systems long times ago, the last two production systems (urban-peri urban and intensive) are viable for higher exotic blood levels of dairy cows. On the other way, crossbreds have dominated the large scale intensive and small-scale dairy sector because of their genetic superiority over local breeds, although the adverse environmental factors may limit their performance. This is because urban-peri urban and intensive production systems following relatively high input and high output system. Purpose of dairy cows production are also different in these production systems. Market oriented production system are being executed in urban-peri urban and intensive systems, while subsistence for the pastoral and crop-livestock mixed agriculture. With challenges of poor nutrition, prevalence of diseases, and limited infrastructure, the number of exotic dairy cows population in Ethiopia is below 5%. Not only the population but also the per head production level is very low; 1.2 liter of milk from local cows and 5-10 liters from the crossed bred dairy. Hence, the country is a net importer of dairy products with import values of nearly 10 million USD"

Use the language of highly acclaimed journals like Taylor and Francis to elaborate on the following . Avoid narrating ideas 
Eurocentrism can be defined as an epistemological framework that situates European culture, history, and values as the defining center of the world, while at the same time disregarding or belittling the contributions of other cultures and civilisations. This ethnocentric positioning perceives the Europe or West as a paragon of universal values and cultural superiority, leading to a systematic undervaluation and misrepresentation of non-European cultures. Consequently, Eurocentric discourse breeds a false dichotomy between the West and non-European societies, perpetuating the notion that the West is superior and in control of all areas of life, including social, political, cultural, scientific, military, and economic fields. Disparaging epithets are often attributed to the “non-West,” reinforcing connotations of inferiority, irrationality, belligerence, otherness, fanaticism, and barbarism in comparison to the West. This paradigm, therefore, not only consolidates the stature of the West, but also perpetuates the subordination of non-European societies within global power structures, and the spheres of knowledge and influence. This belief in Western superiority is often reinforced through various forms of discourse, including history, literature, art, music, etc. In a similar vein, through the utilization of Eurocentric and imperialistic perspectives, academic discourse has consistently depicted ‘Eastern societies’ as exotic and racialized, perpetuating a biased portrayal of the Orient or East.

Generate a commentary for “God” (of Genesis 1:1) that includes only the following information:

The Hebrew word used here for “God,” Elohim, is plural. While elohim may be used to describe multiple deities, OT authors usually use the term to refer to the singular God of Israel (more than 2,000 instances), such as here. Likely the term was a title for the true God but is used so frequently that it becomes viewed as a name.

The narrative begins with a statement assuming the Existence of the Deity. It is not a matter for discussion, argument, or doubt. The Israelite Cosmogony differs in this respect from that of the Babylonians, Phoenicians, Egyptians, &c. The Cosmogonies of the ancients were wont to be preceded by Theogonies. The existence and nativities of the creating divinities were accounted for in mythologies which were often highly complicated, and not seldom grotesque. The Hebrew narrator, by beginning with the Creation, emphasizes his entire freedom from, and exclusion of, polytheistic thought. “God” is infinite; He was before all time. Upon the subject of the Divine Existence prior to the beginning, the writer does not presume to speculate. That Israelite imagination did not wholly avoid the subject, we know from Job 28:25-28, Proverbs 8:22-30, Wis 9:9, Sir 24:9.

Concerning the Israelite conception of God, we learn (1) from the present verse, that He (i) is a Person, and (ii) exists from all eternity; (2) from the whole passage, Genesis 1:1 to Genesis 2:4a, that He is (i) supreme in power, and (ii) perfect in wisdom and goodness. The attribute of power is shewn in creative omnipotence; that of wisdom in the orderly sequence of creation; that of goodness in the benevolent purpose which directed its successive phases.

//@version=4
study(“DAN Trading Strategy”, shorttitle=“DAN_TS”, overlay=true)

// Input settings
input(src=input(close), title=“Source”)
input(ema1Len=input(8), title=“EMA Length 1”)
input(ema2Len=input(21), title=“EMA Length 2”)
input(short=sys_variable.short, long=sys_variable.long, title=“Risk reward ratio”)

// EMA Calculation
ema1 = ema(src, ema1Len)
ema2 = ema(src, ema2Len)

// Dziwne Indicator
A = hl2 - ema1
B = hl2 - ema2
Dziwne = A - B

// QQE Mod Indicator
upper = ema2 + ATR(14) * (input(4))
lower = ema2 - ATR(14) * (input(4))
avg = sma(close, 14)

// SuperTrend Calculation
atr = atr(14)

superTrendLineColor = close > (upper - lower)/2 ? color.green : color.red
plot((upper - lower)/2, title=‘SuperTrend’, color=superTrendLineColor, linewidth=2, style=plot.style_line)

// Flat Market indicator
flatMarket = avg + upper - avg

// Long Position
longCondition = crossover(Dziwne, 0) and close > flatMarket and close[1] > upper[1] and close > upper
if longCondition
    line.new(x1=bar_index[1], y1=hl2[1], x2=bar_index, y2=hl2, color=color.green, width=2)

// Short Position
shortCondition = crossunder(Dziwne, 0) and close < flatMarket and close[1] < upper[1] and close < upper
if shortCondition
    line.new(x1=bar_index[1], y1=hl2[1], x2=bar_index, y2=hl2, color=color.red, width=2)

// Plotting

plot(Dziwne, title=‘Dziwne Indicator’, color=color.white, linewidth=1)

In the west, women left no stone unturned in the construction of the orientalist discourse, even though male scholars made up most of it. There is a ‘persistent trope’ in the West in general and Western feminism in particular, where Third-World women are frequently seen and stereotyped as helpless, submissive, dependent, and oppressive beings. In reality, Western feminists utilized the stereotypical image of women from third-world nations to contribute to the ideological project of the empire and promote the differences between Occidental and Oriental women, the primitive and the civilized, the spiritual and the rational, the weak and the powerful, while dressing up to rescue Third-World women from the plight of patriarchy and in their quest for liberation and development. They boasted about their own political rights and power while acting as agents of progress and enlightenment for Third-World women. According to Abu-Lughod (2002), “projects of saving other women depend on and reinforce a sense of superiority by Westerners, a form of arrogance that deserves to be challenged.” (789) However, even after donning the attire to rescue Third-World women, whom they saw as worse off than themselves, West women frequently “overstepped indigenous women’s subject position and sense of agency.” (Syed and Ali 352)

The MEI index is derived from five factors: sea level pressure, sea surface temperature, zonal and meridional components of surface wind, and outgoing longwave radiation (Wolter & Timlin, 1993). To account for ENSO’s seasonality and minimize the impact of seasonal variations, the MEI index is calculated for 12 overlapping bi-monthly “seasons,” such as December-January, January-February, etc. Based on the MEI index, McPhaden et al. (2006) defined El Niño events as periods when the MEI index remains above 0.5 for at least five consecutive months, while La Niña events are periods with the MEI index below -0.5.
The Integrated Multi-satellite Retrievals for GPM (IMERG) is a data fusion method within the Global Precipitation Measurement (GPM) project that globally calibrates, combines, and interpolates data from all microwave satellite precipitation, infrared satellite precipitation, and other precipitation data sources. The IMERG-Final Run is the research-quality data series within IMERG, starting from June 2000.
Upon acquiring the IMERG-Final Run data, it is resampled to 1°×1° grid to calculate precipitation anomalies. As the monthly oscillations of precipitation obscure the annual oscillations induced by ENSO events, we apply the Z-Score method to monthly standardize anomaly, as presented in Equation 2."
